adds.rationale: The quantum computing wave is explicitly named in the profile as a priority 'next wave' and has zero coverage in the current watchlist; IonQ's 283% YTD gain and ahead-of-schedule AQ 35 milestone in December 2023 confirm the sector entered a meaningful buildup phase, and QTUM (Defiance Quantum ETF, launched September 2018) provides diversified US-listed exposure across hardware, software, and quantum infrastructure without the single-name volatility risk of IONQ alone.

news_evidence.summary: IonQ shares surged 283% year-to-date by December 2023; IonQ achieved its AQ 35 milestone on IonQ Forte in December 2023, a full year ahead of schedule, with the system also made available through Amazon Braket Direct — the strongest commercial-readiness signal yet in the sector.

Seeking Alpha analysis documents quantum computing stocks surging in 2023 on AI-adjacent demand, with IBM, Google, and IonQ all racing toward commercial viability and investor sentiment tracking the AI wave's momentum into quantum.

InvestorPlace December 2023 piece identifies seven quantum computing stocks with 10x return potential, citing Nvidia's DGX Quantum pairing with Quantum Machines hardware and Intel's Tunnel Falls qubit chip as structural markers that quantum is transitioning from research to commercial infrastructure.

removes.rationale: Apple reported its fourth consecutive quarter of year-over-year revenue decline in November 2023 (Mac -34%, iPad -10%, guidance signaling continued weakness in the holiday quarter) and has no wave-specific thesis — no material AI catalyst, SMR exposure, or structural positioning in any of the profile's named waves — making it the watchlist's weakest thematic fit and the natural candidate to free a slot for quantum coverage.

news_evidence.summary: Apple Q4 FY2023: revenue $89.5B (-1% YoY), fourth consecutive quarterly revenue decline; Mac sales -34% YoY, iPad -10%; shares fell more than 3% after-hours on guidance implying no return to growth in the December quarter.

CNBC earnings coverage: Apple CEO Tim Cook noted iPhone 15 was tracking better than iPhone 14, but the quarter marked hardware weakness across Mac and iPad while Services ($22.3B, +16% YoY) remained the only consistent growth segment — insufficient to offset a fourth straight top-line miss.

rationale_overall: The watchlist was at its 8-ticker cap with a structural gap: the profile's second named 'next wave' (quantum computing) had zero coverage while the starter-set placeholder AAPL had deteriorated into the portfolio's weakest thematic link — four consecutive revenue declines, no AI-specific catalyst, and no positioning in any of the profile's named waves. Swapping AAPL for QTUM fills the quantum slot cleanly with a diversified ETF rather than a single high-volatility pure-play, keeping wave-bucket counts well below the 4-ticker cap. All other holdings survived due diligence: NVDA reported +206% revenue growth on AI-accelerated compute demand; GOOGL launched Gemini on December 6; MSFT deepened OpenAI integration at DevDay in November; RKLB secured a $515M government manufacturing contract and set a record with 21 launches at 100% mission success in 2023; BOTZ returned ~39% for the year on AI-and-robotics tailwinds; and ITA remained supported by elevated defense budgets tied to Ukraine and the Israel-Hamas conflict. The nuclear wave (covered by neither NuScale, whose UAMPS project was cancelled November 8, nor any ETF available before the decision date) remains a gap to revisit when a liquid instrument with sufficient history becomes available.
